tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'The basic approach is to simply cast it out and wind it. That is especially true in colder water. Start with a basic steady retrieve, then vary from there.'
Hackney: 'You can pop it and rip it through shallow vegetation, slow-roll it like a spinnerbait down deep, or pitch it under docks. You can fish a swim jig almost anywhere bass live.'
Hackney: 'Use the standard swim jig (not heavy cover) when fishing colder water with less cover.'
Hackney: 'I almost always use braid for swim jigs, whether the water is gin-clear or muddy. Bass aren't fixated on the line with a moving bait.'

Hackney: 'The only time I use fluorocarbon for swim jig fishing is when the water is colder and I need to fish slower and deeper. It has to do with the action of the jig on lighter line, not the fish reacting to the line.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'The trailer is usually a Rage Tail Craw. Sometimes I use a small swimbait if the bass are on shad.'
Hackney: 'I don't believe bass are line shy. You need to adjust what line you use based on how a lure performs using the line, not based on water clarity.'
